> I was thinking that the player subclasses would bring in more people to do stuff that we never imagined to do with mpsyt. I do agree that just giving the url to a script seems promising. But this would give a lot less control to the script user (like the current problem with catt integration).

I agree with you very much. Preserving hackability is important.

I'm not sure subclass approach is a good way to go. If we go for background-running mpv we will introduce a lot of requirements on subclasses, which will be impossible or hard to implement with other players and almost certainly impossible to implement with stuff-that-we-never-imagined-to-do.

I'm afraid this interface will become cumber-stone for both hackability (hard to implement requirements) and better mpv integration (being blocked by subclasses) at the end.

I think we need to start thinking about clearly separating these two usages to have both good integration with mpv and generic extensibility interface.

It would be great to have some feedback from people doing stuff-that-we-never-imagined-to-do with mpv to decide how this interface should look like. Should it be a hook that runs your script? Should it be a python class? Could they use MPRIS to source playback status?

One way or another this should be separate from local playback scenario.

@rob-c There is a fundamental clash between `__getattr__`/`__setattr__` and descriptors. In your recent changes you had to manually call the base class `__setattr__` in order for the descriptor to kick in, this is really a bit of a hack. Really this per-item pre-processing and potential post-precessing on the getting of an item should all be handled *within* the descriptor.

We should have a function which is called by `Descriptor.__set__` at the very beginning which can transform any incoming value (this is basically what the filter does now). Perhaps likewise, the last thing that `Descriptor.__get__` should do before returning a value is pass it though some other transformation function. These functions can have side-effects if we wish as well. The current concept of 'getters' in the schema complicates this a bit as it works very differently to everything else.

If you are using command line, you can run as many time as you want, because every time you re-run a python file, it reset the default graph.
My point was that maybe, if you are using notebooks for example, you run a part of the graph multiple time, so at every run, you add some new ops to the default graph (because the default graph is not reset).

For example, if you want to define 2 same model in a same python file, you need to specify a graph to store them (instead of using the default graph):
```
graph1 = tf.Graph()
with graph1.as_default():
    your_model_for_training
    model.save(...)

graph2 = tf.Graph()
with graph2.as_default():
    your_model_for_testing
    model.load(...)
    model.predict(...)
```

You can use `tf.trainable_variables()` to retrieve all weights, and using their shape, you can calculate your total number of parameters. 

Also note that when you save a model, you also save some other values that are not weights (such as optimizer specific params, moving averages, etc...), if you want to get rid of it, you can load a model with 'load(..., weights_only=True)' to load weights only, and re-save your model (to have a model that only contains weights)

So as I delved into this, I realized a problem with in-line annotations occurs if you want to preserve Python 2.7 compatibility (which I assume you do). If so, you have to resort to the clunky syntax of giving all type information in the form of comments (https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python2.html) and cannot use the nice Python 3 type annotation syntax. But if you use stubs, the stubs can use the nice annotation syntax while Python 2 users still get static type checking.

That pushes me in the direction of using stubs, but what do you think?

That isn't really a pybind problem: `const MatrixXd&` arguments in Eigen involve copying when invoked with a matrix-like object is passed (unless the object actually *is* a MatrixXd, of course).  What you [probably want](https://eigen.tuxfamily.org/dox/TopicFunctionTakingEigenTypes.html#TopicUsingRefClass) is to accept a `const Ref<const MatrixXd>` for a read-only argument, or `Ref<MatrixXd>` for a writeable argument.  (Pybind 2.0 supports them).

You are mistaken, at least for the most part.

Yes, the PK makes up the RDN part of the LDAP obejct. No, the RDN part cannot only contain one field. RDNs are multi-valued and can consist of any number of field=value pairs. E.g., `uid=nik+cn=Dominik George` is a perfectly valid RDN, consisting of the `cn` and `uid` attributes of the object.

From RFC 4514:

```
      RelativeDistinguishedName ::= SET SIZE (1..MAX) OF
          AttributeTypeAndValue

      AttributeTypeAndValue ::= SEQUENCE {
          type  AttributeType,
          value AttributeValue }
```

Desktop streams a hybrid RTMP/HTTP streaming protocol is used. The RTMP server is used to give out metadata about the channel, including a list of streams and a base url for all of the streams. Each of the streams has metadata, including bit rate, resolution. 

The streams are divided in to chunks (or segments), each chunk is found under the base URL for the channel. The exact path for each chunk is given as a combination of a file pattern, chunkId, and token.  

For example, these are some fields from a stream entry.

```python
{
    'isTranscoded': False, 
    'streamName': u'streams/live_1_%_%.flv', 
    'chunkTime': 5000.0, 
    'height': 720.0, 
    'chunkRange': {u'1475815790': u'797895358', u'1475815780': u'640805711'}, 
    'offset': 681.0, 
    'chunkId': 1475815783.0, 
    'bitrate': 1513.0, 
    'offsetInMs': 4985.0
}
```

The important fields are `chunkRange`, `streamName`, and `chunkId`. `chunkRange` tells the client the "tokens" that will be used for a range of `chunkId`s, and `chunkId` tells the client the current chunk that is available. 

In the example stream we have chunk tokens for 1475815780 and 1475815790, and the current chunkId is 1475815783
Using the streamName the client can determine the urls for the chunks 1475815780-1475815790, but that only up to chunk 1475815783 exists
 - `streams/live_1_1475815780_640805711.flv`
 - `streams/live_1_1475815781_640805711.flv`
 - `streams/live_1_1475815782_640805711.flv`
 - `streams/live_1_1475815783_640805711.flv`


Subsequent requests to the moduleInfo endpoint may return a more recent chunkId, but with the same chunkRange.

```python
{
    ...
    'chunkId': 1475815787.0, 
    ...
}
```

This indicates to the client that chunks up to 1475815787 are avilable (`streams/live_1_1475815784_640805711.flv ... streams/live_1_1475815787_640805711.flv`).
Further requests will update the current chunkId, when the current chunkId crosses the boundary in a chunk range the token will change. 

eg. `streams/live_1_1475815787_640805711.flv ... streams/live_1_1475815793_797895358.flv`

There is a bug in the current implementation where some segments can be missed, which can lead to chopping/skipping playback.

Hey 2015E8007061068, thanks for the report.

It looks like this is coming from rasterio, one of our dependencies -- a minor bug [in their setup.py](https://github.com/mapbox/rasterio/blob/994def271a14126e690a080ead816626a70fdeb0/setup.py#L134), when displaying the following error message:

    A GDAL API version must be specified. Provide a path 
    to gdal-config using a GDAL_CONFIG environment variable 
    or use a GDAL_VERSION environment variable.

To get around it, you likely need to install gdal or point one of those environment variables to an existing install. Otherwise, [we recommend conda ](https://datacube-core.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ops/conda.html) as a much easier install method than straight pip (which bundles libraries like gdal).

A few additional thoughts:
- My thought on passing the nursery a list of functions would be that all nursery functions would be passed (and required to accept) the nursery as their first argument (which they could always ignore).  Perhaps even an object oriented approach where we pass classes or instances and the nursery would be accessible through self.nursery.  Although this gets away from some of the nice simplicity.
- I am not sure I am sold on awaiting server startup being a special enough case to break the full-time parenting rule.  Are we really that confident a poorly written `handler` wouldn't enter `while True:` loop and happily accept connections while never allowing `__aexit__` to reap the tasks of closed connections?
- I am surprised your second example is potentially too convoluted as I thought it was somewhat the textbook approach :-)
- Not sure if you saw my pull request on h11 (https://github.com/njsmith/h11/pull/46) but I took a rough stab at changing the curio example to support a very simple trio tcp_server

That's not flexibility, that's enabling a bad practice. I cannot think of a reason why would someone want to save images in the database, especially when these images are obtained from a text editor. This is just a recipe for disaster. So I am against it unless I hear specific, valid arguments to convince me otherwise.

As for the `MEDIA`, you can set your media directory on S3, or use multiple embedded Django projects to handle different needs. Otherwise, you can inherit `forms.ImageForm` , change it, and use it to save the contents in a specific widget. 

On that point, we can probably make this slightly easier by allow the use of a function as well as a string for names. So, that's something we can work on.

On the topics of the `--min-cpu` and `--min-ram` flags, I had previously suggested that we might just follow the lead of the Pipelines API v2 and only accept the machine type. I am now leaning toward implementing a fairly simple deterministic handling of these flags that I think will ease migration.

I propose that rather than try to do exactly what the the v1alpha2 API (which tried a best fit to predefined machine types) had done, we should do a best fit for a custom machine type. (Note that Custom Machine Types did not exist when v1alpha2 was created.)

Rules around custom machine shapes can be found here:
https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/creating-instance-with-custom-machine-type

We have two special cases not handled by custom machine types: the shared core instances. So I propose roughly:

if --min-cores <= 0.2 and --min-ram <= 0.60
  machine-type = f1-micro
else if --min-cores <= 0.5 and --min-ram <= 1.70
  machine-type = g1-small

else:

  cores = 2^n where 2^n <= --min-cores
  ram = cores x 0.9 GB

  But if the amount of RAM is greater than 6.5 x cores, then increase the number of cores.

This is already implemented by Cromwell (without the support for the shared core instances):

https://github.com/broadinstitute/cromwell/blob/96b849c09def273e5e764ced403ee03fc413f1e0/supportedBackends/google/pipelines/v2alpha1/src/main/scala/cromwell/backend/google/pipelines/v2alpha1/MachineConstraints.scala

JsExtension创建过程代码重构和优化

```python
class JsExtension(object):
    def __init__(self, extension_installation_path):
        self.registry = read_a_map_registry(
            os.path.join(extension_installation_path, JS_EXTENSION_REGISTRY))
        self.home = os.path.join(
            extension_installation_path,
            self.registry[REGISTRY_JS_FOLDER])

def read_a_map_registry(registry_json):
    with codecs.open(registry_json, 'r', 'utf-8') as f:
        content = f.read()
        return json.loads(content)
```
目前有两个缺点：

- 可测性不好
- 对象创建和初始化区分不清

建议两点修改

```python
class JsExtension(object):
    def __init__(self, extension_installation_path, registry):
        self.registry = registry
        self.home = os.path.join(extension_installation_path,  self.registry[REGISTRY_JS_FOLDER])

    @classmethod
    def from_registry_path(extension_installation_path):
       path = os.path.join(extension_installation_path, JS_EXTENSION_REGISTRY)
        with codecs.open(registry_json, 'r', 'utf-8') as f:
            content = f.read()
            registry = json.loads(content)
            return cls(extension_installation_path, registry)
```

- 构建函数 `extension_installation_path` 文件路径变更为表示其文件内容的字典 `registry_dict`
- `read_a_map_registry` 作为 `JsExtension` 的classmethod 函数，功能上是创建函数

优点：

- 当 registry.json 不存在或验证不通过时，现在不会创建一个具体的 JsExtension 实例，符合实际场景
- 接上述情况，错误栈由 “read_a_map_registry => __init__  => 上层调用” 缩短为 “from_registry_path => 上层调用”，表明是对象创建过程中出现错误，与初始化过程无关
- 对于特定的 registry 字典，目前可以传入 `registry` 参数进行测试，不必对 `read_a_map_registry` 进行 mock 测试
